title: The Saite Reunification
bullets: Smendes I founded the Twenty-first Dynasty at Tanis around 1069 BCE, splitting Egypt between a northern pharaoh and the High Priest of Amun ruling Upper Egypt from Thebes.
The Tanis royal tombs, discovered by Pierre Montet in 1939, contained gold funerary masks rivaling Tutankhamun's but received almost no attention because Germany invaded Poland the same month.
The Libyan pharaoh Shoshenq I founded the Twenty-second Dynasty around 943 BCE and sacked cities in Israel and Judah, recorded on the Bubastite Portal at Karnak.
The Kushite king Piye conquered all of Egypt around 747 BCE, founding the Twenty-fifth Dynasty; his Victory Stela reveals a conqueror more concerned with ritual purity and horse welfare than plunder.
Shabaka ordered the Memphite Theology recopied onto the Shabaka Stone, preserving a creation narrative in which the god Ptah creates the world through thought and speech.
Taharqa was the most prolific builder of the Twenty-fifth Dynasty, erecting a massive colonnade at Karnak whose single surviving column stands nearly 70 feet tall.
In 663 BCE Ashurbanipal's Assyrian army sacked Thebes, plundering centuries of accumulated temple treasure from what had been the richest city in the ancient world.

Psamtik I, installed as an Assyrian puppet, spent fifteen years quietly dismantling Persian control and reunifying Egypt, recruiting Greek hoplite mercenaries and installing his daughter Nitocris as God's Wife of Amun at Thebes.

The Saite Renaissance was a deliberate effort to reconnect with the Old Kingdom over two thousand years in the past, with artists copying Old Kingdom reliefs while achieving a technical polish the originals never matched.

title: Egypt Looks Outward
bullets: Necho II marched north to rescue the dying Assyrian Empire, calculating that a weakened Assyria was preferable to a triumphant Babylon controlling the entire Fertile Crescent.
At the Battle of Megiddo in 609 BCE, Necho II defeated the Judean army and killed King Josiah, ending Judah's brief independence and imposing a puppet king.
The Battle of Carchemish in 605 BCE was a decisive Babylonian victory that drove Egypt from the Levant permanently; Egypt would never again hold significant territory east of the Sinai.
Necho II began the Canal of the Pharaohs, a roughly 100-mile waterway linking the Nile to the Red Sea, a forerunner of the Suez Canal built 2,400 years before Ferdinand de Lesseps.
Necho II commissioned Phoenician sailors to circumnavigate Africa in a three-year voyage, over two thousand years before Vasco da Gama rounded the Cape of Good Hope.
Psamtik II campaigned deep into Nubia around 593 BCE and systematically erased the names of the Twenty-fifth Dynasty Kushite pharaohs from Egyptian monuments.
Greek and Carian mercenaries carved the Abu Simbel graffiti on Ramesses II's colossal statues, among the earliest surviving Greek inscriptions anywhere.
Apries's failed expedition to support the Greek colony of Cyrene triggered a military mutiny, and the general Amasis seized the throne after defeating the pharaoh in battle.

title: The Last Great Pharaohs
bullets: Amasis II, a non-royal military officer proclaimed pharaoh by mutinous soldiers, became one of Egypt's most capable rulers and reigned for forty-four years.
Amasis reorganized Naucratis as the sole authorized Greek trading port in Egypt, a managed-trade model that anticipated European treaty ports by over two thousand years.
Saite sculpture reached its technical peak under Amasis, with artists copying Old Kingdom models in hard stones like basalt and greywacke while achieving a polish the originals never matched.

Egyptian art influenced Greek art in the seventh and sixth centuries BCE; the early Greek kouros statue bears a striking resemblance to Egyptian standing figures in pose, proportions, and the left-foot-forward stance.
The demotic script reached its mature form during this period, giving Egypt three simultaneous writing systems: hieroglyphs for monuments, hieratic for priests, and demotic for everything else.
Pythagoras reportedly spent years studying geometry and the doctrine of transmigration of souls with Egyptian priests before founding his philosophical school in Italy.
Egyptian medicine was more specialized than anything the Greek world had yet produced; Herodotus described doctors each dedicated to a different disease.
Cambyses II of Persia invaded Egypt in 525 BCE after Amasis's alliance network with Polycrates of Samos, Lydia, and Babylon had been systematically destroyed by Cyrus the Great.

title: Under the Persian Yoke
bullets: At the Battle of Pelusium in 525 BCE, Cambyses II defeated Psamtik III and made Egypt a satrapy of the Persian Empire, ending over a century of Saite independence.
The Egyptian sources paint Cambyses as a madman who killed the sacred Apis bull, but contemporary evidence, including the Udjahorresnet inscription, suggests a more nuanced reality.
The Lost Army of Cambyses, fifty thousand soldiers allegedly swallowed by a sandstorm while marching to attack the Oracle of Amun at Siwa, remains one of antiquity's great unsolved mysteries.
Darius I completed the Canal of the Pharaohs linking the Nile to the Red Sea and built the Temple of Hibis at Kharga Oasis, the best-preserved Persian-period temple in Egypt.
The Inaros Revolt of 460 BCE drew in an Athenian fleet of two hundred triremes, briefly seizing Memphis before Persia crushed the rebellion and virtually annihilated the Athenian force.
The Elephantine Papyri document a Jewish military colony at Elephantine with its own temple to Yahweh, revealing a cosmopolitan multicultural society under Persian rule.
Herodotus visited Egypt around 450 BCE and devoted the entire second book of his Histories to Egyptian customs, producing the most detailed outsider description of Egypt before the modern era.
Egypt's 365-day civil calendar with twelve 30-day months plus five epagomenal days was the most practical calendar in the ancient world and would later feed into Julius Caesar's calendar reform.

title: The Last Independence
bullets: Amyrtaeus of Sais expelled the Persian garrison in 404 BCE, becoming the sole pharaoh of the Twenty-eighth Dynasty and restoring native rule after over 120 years.
The Twenty-ninth Dynasty maintained Egyptian independence for twenty years primarily through diplomacy, trading grain to Athens and Greek city-states for military and diplomatic support.
Plato reportedly visited Egypt around 390 BCE and was deeply impressed, later crediting Egyptian priests with the myth of Atlantis and ideas about the immortality of the soul.
Nectanebo I repelled a massive Persian invasion in 373 BCE using Delta geography, the annual Nile flood, and quarreling between the Persian general Pharnabazus and the Athenian Iphicrates.

The Temple of Isis at Philae, founded by Nectanebo I, would become the last functioning temple of the ancient Egyptian religion, operating until 550 CE, over nineteen centuries after the first pharaohs.

The mammisi (birth houses) of the Thirtieth Dynasty formalized divine birth narratives architecturally, establishing a theological framework flexible enough to accommodate Libyan, Nubian, Persian, and Greek rulers.
Nectanebo II was the last native-born pharaoh of Egypt; after his defeat by Artaxerxes III in 343 BCE, no person of Egyptian birth would rule Egypt independently for over two thousand years.

The Romance of Alexander later claimed Nectanebo II fled to Macedonia and magically fathered Alexander the Great, a fiction reflecting the psychological need for unbroken continuity in legitimate kingship.

title: Second Persian Period and Alexander
bullets: The Second Persian Period lasted only eleven years but was harsh enough that when Alexander the Great arrived in 332 BCE, Egyptians greeted him as a liberator.
Alexander was crowned pharaoh at Memphis and sacrificed to the Apis bull, publicly honoring the sacred animal that the Persians had allegedly desecrated.

Alexander marched 350 miles across the Libyan Desert to the Oracle of Amun at Siwa, where the priest reportedly greeted him as son of Zeus-Ammon, granting him legitimacy in both Egyptian and Greek religious frameworks.

Alexander founded Alexandria in January 331 BCE on a Mediterranean coastal strip, facing outward toward Greece rather than inward toward the Nile Valley, announcing Egypt's future as a Mediterranean power.
Ptolemy I Soter seized Egypt after Alexander's death in 323 BCE and founded the Ptolemaic Dynasty, which would rule for nearly three hundred years until Cleopatra VII's death in 30 BCE.

The Library of Alexandria, founded around 283 BCE, housed hundreds of thousands of scrolls and attracted scholars from across the Mediterranean; the Lighthouse of Alexandria stood roughly 330 feet tall.
The Rosetta Stone, a 196 BCE decree of Ptolemy V inscribed in hieroglyphs, demotic, and Greek, would provide the key to deciphering hieroglyphs when discovered in 1799.

Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth at Alexandria by comparing the sun's angle at noon in Alexandria and Syene, using Egyptian geography that provided two cities on a known north-south line.

title: Egyptian Culture in the Late Period
bullets: The Late Period saw an animal cult explosion: at the ibis catacombs at Saqqara alone, archaeologists have found an estimated four million mummified ibises; X-ray analysis reveals many mummies are fraudulent.
The Serapeum at Saqqara housed massive granite sarcophagi weighing up to 70 tons each, containing mummified Apis bulls interred with full royal honors.
Auguste Mariette discovered the Serapeum in 1851 by following clues in Strabo's ancient description of a sphinx-lined avenue leading to the burial complex.

Imhotep, architect of the Step Pyramid around 2650 BCE, was deified in the Late Period as a god of medicine; the Greeks identified him with Asclepius, creating a syncretic healing cult that spread across the Hellenistic world.
The House of Life at every major temple functioned as the ancient equivalent of a university library, compiling texts on medicine, astronomy, mathematics, and ritual.

Late Period sculpture combined faithful Old Kingdom poses with unprecedented naturalistic portraiture; the Brooklyn Black Head and Green Head portraits achieve an almost unsettling realism in hard-stone carving.
The demotic literary tradition produced wisdom texts, narrative fiction, and legal compilations; demotic appeared alongside hieroglyphs and Greek on the Rosetta Stone.
Late Period temple hieroglyphs grew more elaborate and deliberately encoded, used by priests as a badge of specialized identity in a world increasingly dominated by Greek.

title: Legacy
bullets: Every conqueror who took Egypt adopted the pharaonic system because the temple network was the administrative infrastructure and the pharaonic role was the operating interface; without them, Egypt was ungovernable.

Egypt would become Rome's most vital province, shipping millions of bushels of grain annually; the Romans forbade senators from visiting Egypt without imperial permission, fearing anyone who controlled the grain could hold Rome hostage.
The Saite Renaissance was not mere imitation but a creative reengagement that produced sculpture technically superior to anything Egypt had made before, in hard stones polished to a mirror finish.

The last datable hieroglyphic inscription, the Graffito of Esmet-Akhom, was carved at Philae on August 24, 394 CE; the writing system remained undecipherable for nearly fifteen hundred years until Champollion cracked the code in 1822.

Coptic, the final stage of the Egyptian language written in a modified Greek alphabet, survived as the liturgical language of the Coptic Christian Church and provided the key Champollion needed to read hieroglyphs.
The cult of Isis spread across the Roman Mediterranean as one of the most popular mystery religions; the image of Isis nursing Horus bears a structural resemblance to the Christian Madonna and Child.
Egyptian civilization in continuous, recognizable form lasted more than three and a half millennia, far longer than Mesopotamian cuneiform, the Indus script, or Mycenaean Linear B.

The Egyptians built in stone because stone lasts; the Saite artists who copied Old Kingdom models were not just nostalgic but archivists, making sure the record survived what they knew the world was trying to erase.
